ucs.title: [LLM] Deep Panda / Shell Crew: IIS web shell (China Chopper) post-exploit recon chain
ucs.description: Hunts the Shell Crew signature pattern of a w3wp.exe (or other web-server) process spawning cmd.exe/powershell.exe — often with hidden-window flags — followed by a burst of native reconnaissance binaries. This is the operational fingerprint Deep Panda left at Anthem and across Shell Crew intrusions where China Chopper / .aspx web shells were dropped as the initial foothold.
ucs.splunk:
| tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name IN ("w3wp.exe","httpd.exe","tomcat*.exe","nginx.exe","php-cgi.exe") AND Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","powershell_ise.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","tasklist.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","quser.exe","netstat.exe","wmic.exe","reg.exe","nltest.exe","sc.exe","ping.exe") by Processes.dest Processes.user Processes.parent_process Processes.parent_process_name Processes.process_name Processes.process Processes.process_id Processes.parent_process_id
| `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)`
| eval is_hidden=if(match(process,"(?i)(-w(in)?\s*(1|hidden)|-windowstyle\s+hidden|-nop|-enc|-encodedcommand)"),1,0)
| eval is_recon=if(match(process_name,"(?i)(net1?\.exe|whoami|tasklist|ipconfig|systeminfo|quser|netstat|wmic|nltest|ping)"),1,0)
| stats min(firstTime) as firstTime max(lastTime) as lastTime values(process_name) as child_procs values(process) as cmdlines dc(process_name) as distinct_children sum(is_hidden) as hidden_count sum(is_recon) as recon_count by dest user parent_process_name parent_process
| where (distinct_children >= 2 AND recon_count >= 2) OR hidden_count > 0
| `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
let WebSvc = dynamic(["w3wp.exe","httpd.exe","tomcat.exe","nginx.exe","php-cgi.exe"]);
let Recon  = dynamic(["net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","tasklist.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","quser.exe","netstat.exe","wmic.exe","reg.exe","nltest.exe","sc.exe","ping.exe"]);
let Shells = dynamic(["cmd.exe","powershell.exe","powershell_ise.exe","pwsh.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(1d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ (WebSvc)
| where FileName in~ (Shells) or FileName in~ (Recon)
| extend HiddenWin = ProcessCommandLine matches regex @"(?i)(-w(in)?\s*(1|hidden)|-windowstyle\s+hidden|-nop|-enc|-encodedcommand)"
| extend IsRecon  = FileName in~ (Recon)
| summarize FirstSeen=min(Timestamp), LastSeen=max(Timestamp),
            ChildProcs=make_set(FileName,50),
            CmdLines=make_set(ProcessCommandLine,50),
            DistinctChildren=dcount(FileName),
            HiddenCount=countif(HiddenWin),
            ReconCount=countif(IsRecon)
          by DeviceName, AccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessId, InitiatingProcessFolderPath
| where (DistinctChildren >= 2 and ReconCount >= 2) or HiddenCount > 0
| order by LastSeen desc
ucs.rationale: A legitimate IIS worker (w3wp.exe) almost never spawns cmd/PowerShell, and certainly not a recon burst. This exact pattern — China Chopper .aspx → w3wp child shell → net/tasklist/ipconfig/wmic enumeration — is documented Shell Crew/Deep Panda tradecraft (RSA Shell Crew, Mandiant, CrowdStrike Black Vine reporting on Anthem). The hidden-window PowerShell flag set (`-w hidden -nop -enc`) is a Deep Pa

ucs.title: [LLM] Deep Panda Sticky-Keys / Accessibility Features debugger backdoor (T1546.008)
ucs.description: Hunts modification of the Image File Execution Options 'Debugger' value for accessibility binaries (sethc.exe, utilman.exe, osk.exe, Magnify.exe, Narrator.exe, DisplaySwitch.exe, atbroker.exe) — Deep Panda's signature pre-auth RDP persistence/escalation primitive. Pressing Shift x5 or Win+U at the lock screen then yields SYSTEM cmd.exe.
ucs.splunk:
| tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Registry where (Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\sethc.exe*" OR Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\utilman.exe*" OR Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\osk.exe*" OR Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\Magnify.exe*" OR Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\Narrator.exe*" OR Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\DisplaySwitch.exe*" OR Registry.registry_path="*\\Image File Execution Options\\atbroker.exe*") AND Registry.registry_value_name="Debugger" by Registry.dest Registry.user Registry.process_name Registry.process_id Registry.registry_path Registry.registry_key_name Registry.registry_value_data
| `drop_dm_object_name(Registry)`
| eval evil=if(match(registry_value_data,"(?i)(cmd\.exe|powershell|pwsh|\\\\temp\\\\|\\\\public\\\\|\\\\programdata\\\\|\.bat|\.vbs|\.hta|net\.exe)"),1,0)
| where evil=1
| `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
| table firstTime lastTime dest user process_name registry_path registry_value_data
ucs.kql:
DeviceRegistryEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where ActionType in ("RegistryValueSet","RegistryKeyCreated")
| where RegistryKey has @"Image File Execution Options"
| where RegistryKey matches regex @"(?i)\\(sethc|utilman|osk|magnify|narrator|displayswitch|atbroker)\.exe$"
| where RegistryValueName =~ "Debugger"
| where isnotempty(RegistryValueData)
| extend Suspicious = RegistryValueData matches regex @"(?i)(cmd\.exe|powershell|pwsh|\\temp\\|\\public\\|\\programdata\\|\.bat|\.vbs|\.hta|net\.exe)"
| where Suspicious or RegistryValueData !endswith ".exe" or RegistryValueData has_any ("\\Users\\Public","\\Windows\\Temp")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, InitiatingProcessAccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, RegistryKey, RegistryValueName, RegistryValueData, ActionType
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.rationale: Setting an IFEO Debugger on sethc/utilman is essentially never legitimate — and it is the specific persistence/escalation method publicly attributed to Shell Crew/Deep Panda by RSA and Mandiant (combined with their habit of RDPing into edge servers after web-shell access). It pairs naturally with the actor's known T1021.002/T1018 lateral movement tradecraft and is far more actor-specific than a ge
